texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Abstract
texts.text: Abstract

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Maximal Extractable Value (MEV) remains a structural threat to blockchain fairness because the block producer can often observe pending transactions and unilaterally decide their ordering or inclusion. Existing mitigations hide transaction contents or outsource ordering, but they typically leave two gaps unresolved: commitments are not authenticated by slashable identities, and inclusion obligations are not backed by transferable evidence that other validators can verify.
texts.text: Maximal Extractable Value (MEV) remains a structural threat to blockchain fairness because the block producer can often observe pending transactions and unilaterally decide their ordering or inclusion. Existing mitigations hide transaction contents or outsource ordering, but they typically leave two gaps unresolved: commitments are not authenticated by slashable identities, and inclusion obligations are not backed by transferable evidence that other validators can verify.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: This paper presents MEV-ACE , a fair-ordering protocol for proposer-controlled ordering MEV . MEV-ACE combines three mechanisms: (1) registered economic identities , whose authentication keys are deterministically derived from the ACEGF framework and bonded on-chain; (2) authenticated commit/open messages with validator receipt thresholds , which make admissibility and inclusion obligations independently auditable; and (3) verifiable-delay randomness , which determines transaction order only after the admissible commitment set is fixed.
texts.text: This paper presents MEV-ACE , a fair-ordering protocol for proposer-controlled ordering MEV . MEV-ACE combines three mechanisms: (1) registered economic identities , whose authentication keys are deterministically derived from the ACEGF framework and bonded on-chain; (2) authenticated commit/open messages with validator receipt thresholds , which make admissibility and inclusion obligations independently auditable; and (3) verifiable-delay randomness , which determines transaction order only after the admissible commitment set is fixed.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We formalize the protocol in a 3 f +1 validator model with threshold receipts and show three properties under standard assumptions: order-unpredictability once the admissible set is locked, commitment authenticity under EUF-CMA security of the authentication signature scheme, and accountable inclusion for transactions that obtain threshold commit and open receipts. Under these conditions, and when producer/user bonds exceed the one-slot gain from invalid execution or selective non-opening, MEV-ACE removes unilateral proposer discretion over front-running, sandwiching, and censorship against admitted transactions. The protocol remains single-slot in structure, requires no threshold decryption committee, and is compatible with post-quantum signatures such as ML-DSA-44.
texts.text: We formalize the protocol in a 3 f +1 validator model with threshold receipts and show three properties under standard assumptions: order-unpredictability once the admissible set is locked, commitment authenticity under EUF-CMA security of the authentication signature scheme, and accountable inclusion for transactions that obtain threshold commit and open receipts. Under these conditions, and when producer/user bonds exceed the one-slot gain from invalid execution or selective non-opening, MEV-ACE removes unilateral proposer discretion over front-running, sandwiching, and censorship against admitted transactions. The protocol remains single-slot in structure, requires no threshold decryption committee, and is compatible with post-quantum signatures such as ML-DSA-44.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 1 Introduction
texts.text: 1 Introduction

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Maximal Extractable Value (MEV) refers to the profit a block producer can extract by manipulating the ordering, inclusion, or censorship of transactions within a block [2]. Large-scale empirical studies and measurement efforts have documented substantial extracted value on Ethereum and related DeFi ecosystems [3, 4], imposing a regressive tax on ordinary users while concentrating profit among sophisticated searchers and cooperating validators.
texts.text: Maximal Extractable Value (MEV) refers to the profit a block producer can extract by manipulating the ordering, inclusion, or censorship of transactions within a block [2]. Large-scale empirical studies and measurement efforts have documented substantial extracted value on Ethereum and related DeFi ecosystems [3, 4], imposing a regressive tax on ordinary users while concentrating profit among sophisticated searchers and cooperating validators.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The MEV problem is structural: in conventional blockchains, the block producer observes pending transactions in cleartext and has unilateral discretion over their ordering. This asymmetry enables three canonical attack classes:
texts.text: The MEV problem is structural: in conventional blockchains, the block producer observes pending transactions in cleartext and has unilateral discretion over their ordering. This asymmetry enables three canonical attack classes:

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Front-running : The producer inserts a transaction before a target transaction to capture a price movement.
texts.text: Front-running : The producer inserts a transaction before a target transaction to capture a price movement.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Sandwich attacks : The producer brackets a target transaction with a buy-before and sell-after pair, extracting the slippage.
texts.text: Sandwich attacks : The producer brackets a target transaction with a buy-before and sell-after pair, extracting the slippage.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Censorship : The producer selectively excludes transactions to disadvantage specific users or benefit competing transactions.
texts.text: Censorship : The producer selectively excludes transactions to disadvantage specific users or benefit competing transactions.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We collectively term these ordering-based MEV , distinguished from information-based MEV (e.g., cross-domain arbitrage from public oracle updates) which does not depend on the producer's ordering power.
texts.text: We collectively term these ordering-based MEV , distinguished from information-based MEV (e.g., cross-domain arbitrage from public oracle updates) which does not depend on the producer's ordering power.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 1.1 Limitations of Existing Approaches
texts.text: 1.1 Limitations of Existing Approaches

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Prior work has pursued four main strategies, each with fundamental limitations:
texts.text: Prior work has pursued four main strategies, each with fundamental limitations:

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Commit-reveal schemes [5]. Users hide transaction contents during an initial commitment phase and reveal later. This reduces early information leakage, but by itself does not authenticate who is allowed to create admissible commitments, nor does it provide a portable proof that an omitted commitment had to be included.
texts.text: Commit-reveal schemes [5]. Users hide transaction contents during an initial commitment phase and reveal later. This reduces early information leakage, but by itself does not authenticate who is allowed to create admissible commitments, nor does it provide a portable proof that an omitted commitment had to be included.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Threshold encryption [6]. Transactions are encrypted under a threshold key and decrypted only after ordering is fixed. This requires a trusted decryption committee, introduces at least one full round-trip of latency for threshold decryption, and remains vulnerable to producer-committee collusion.
texts.text: Threshold encryption [6]. Transactions are encrypted under a threshold key and decrypted only after ordering is fixed. This requires a trusted decryption committee, introduces at least one full round-trip of latency for threshold decryption, and remains vulnerable to producer-committee collusion.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Fair-ordering services [7]. Decentralized oracles provide ordering attestations. These introduce an external trust assumption and oracle-layer latency, and they shift, rather than remove, the requirement that some outside party honestly witness and enforce ordering.
texts.text: Fair-ordering services [7]. Decentralized oracles provide ordering attestations. These introduce an external trust assumption and oracle-layer latency, and they shift, rather than remove, the requirement that some outside party honestly witness and enforce ordering.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Proposer-builder separation (PBS) [8]. Separates block building from block proposing. This redistributes MEV rather than eliminating it: the builder still extracts orderingbased MEV, and the proposer receives a share via auction.
texts.text: Proposer-builder separation (PBS) [8]. Separates block building from block proposing. This redistributes MEV rather than eliminating it: the builder still extracts orderingbased MEV, and the proposer receives a share via auction.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 1.2 The Authenticated-Admissibility Gap
texts.text: 1.2 The Authenticated-Admissibility Gap

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We identify a structural weakness shared by these approaches: the protocol does not natively authenticate admissible commitments by registered, slashable identities, nor does it require inclusion evidence that can be verified by any validator . This enables the following attacks:
texts.text: We identify a structural weakness shared by these approaches: the protocol does not natively authenticate admissible commitments by registered, slashable identities, nor does it require inclusion evidence that can be verified by any validator . This enables the following attacks:

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 1. Commitment stuffing : The producer generates many low-cost commitments to dilute the ordering pool and improve the expected placement of its own trades.
texts.text: Commitment stuffing : The producer generates many low-cost commitments to dilute the ordering pool and improve the expected placement of its own trades.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 2. Selective non-opening : The producer admits many candidate transactions and reveals only the subset whose realized positions are profitable.
texts.text: Selective non-opening : The producer admits many candidate transactions and reveals only the subset whose realized positions are profitable.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 3. Unprovable omission : Honest users may have broadcast valid commitments or openings, but other validators lack a transferable proof that the producer was obligated to include them.
texts.text: Unprovable omission : Honest users may have broadcast valid commitments or openings, but other validators lack a transferable proof that the producer was obligated to include them.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: These attacks are especially problematic in systems where identity creation is cheap, commitment admission is not authenticated, and omission can only be detected by local observation rather than by globally verifiable receipts.
texts.text: These attacks are especially problematic in systems where identity creation is cheap, commitment admission is not authenticated, and omission can only be detected by local observation rather than by globally verifiable receipts.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 1.3 Our Contributions
texts.text: 1.3 Our Contributions

texts.label: text
texts.orig: This paper makes the following contributions:
texts.text: This paper makes the following contributions:

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 1. Formal model of admissible fair ordering. We define proposer-controlled ordering MEV in a model where commitments and openings become mandatory only after they collect threshold validator receipts, and formalize three security properties: order-unpredictability, commitment authenticity, and accountable inclusion (Section 3).
texts.text: Formal model of admissible fair ordering. We define proposer-controlled ordering MEV in a model where commitments and openings become mandatory only after they collect threshold validator receipts, and formalize three security properties: order-unpredictability, commitment authenticity, and accountable inclusion (Section 3).

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 2. The MEV-ACE protocol. We present a single-slot protocol that combines ACEGF-derived authentication keys, bonded identity registration, threshold receipt certificates for commit/open phases, and VDF-based delayed randomness (Section 4).
texts.text: The MEV-ACE protocol. We present a single-slot protocol that combines ACEGF-derived authentication keys, bonded identity registration, threshold receipt certificates for commit/open phases, and VDF-based delayed randomness (Section 4).

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 3. Security analysis. We prove that MEV-ACE satisfies all three properties under the sequentiality of the VDF, EUF-CMA security of the authentication signature scheme, collision resistance of SHA-256, and standard BFT delivery assumptions for omission proofs. We further derive explicit economic conditions under which honest execution is the producer's best response (Section 5).
texts.text: Security analysis. We prove that MEV-ACE satisfies all three properties under the sequentiality of the VDF, EUF-CMA security of the authentication signature scheme, collision resistance of SHA-256, and standard BFT delivery assumptions for omission proofs. We further derive explicit economic conditions under which honest execution is the producer's best response (Section 5).

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 4. Deployment guidance. We provide a parameterized latency and communication analysis that makes the timing assumptions explicit, instead of conflating VDF security parameters with wall-clock runtime (Section 6).
texts.text: Deployment guidance. We provide a parameterized latency and communication analysis that makes the timing assumptions explicit, instead of conflating VDF security parameters with wall-clock runtime (Section 6).

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 5. Identity-authenticated ordering architecture. We show how ACE-GF can be used as an identity-native key-derivation layer for MEV protection without requiring seed storage or threshold decryption, while keeping the actual security boundary at the protocol layer: registered keys, bonded participation, and independently verifiable receipts.
texts.text: Identity-authenticated ordering architecture. We show how ACE-GF can be used as an identity-native key-derivation layer for MEV protection without requiring seed storage or threshold decryption, while keeping the actual security boundary at the protocol layer: registered keys, bonded participation, and independently verifiable receipts.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 1.4 Scope and Non-Goals
texts.text: 1.4 Scope and Non-Goals

texts.label: text
texts.orig: MEV-ACE targets proposer-controlled ordering MEV on a single chain. Informationbased MEV-such as back-running public oracle price updates or cross-domain statistical arbitrage-is not in scope. These forms of MEV exploit publicly available information and do not disappear merely because proposer ordering discretion is reduced; they require orthogonal mitigations such as batch auctions, delayed disclosure, or private execution.
texts.text: MEV-ACE targets proposer-controlled ordering MEV on a single chain. Informationbased MEV-such as back-running public oracle price updates or cross-domain statistical arbitrage-is not in scope. These forms of MEV exploit publicly available information and do not disappear merely because proposer ordering discretion is reduced; they require orthogonal mitigations such as batch auctions, delayed disclosure, or private execution.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2.1 ACE-GF Identity Framework
texts.text: 2.1 ACE-GF Identity Framework

texts.label: text
texts.orig: ACE-GF (Atomic Cryptographic Entity Generative Framework) [1] is a deterministic key-derivation framework that derives application-specific cryptographic material from an identity root called the Root Entropy Value ( REV ). For MEV-ACE, the relevant properties are:
texts.text: ACE-GF (Atomic Cryptographic Entity Generative Framework) [1] is a deterministic key-derivation framework that derives application-specific cryptographic material from an identity root called the Root Entropy Value ( REV ). For MEV-ACE, the relevant properties are:

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Deterministic derivation. Application keys are derived under explicit context strings, allowing the protocol to dedicate a distinct authentication key to MEV ordering without reusing keys from unrelated applications.
texts.text: Deterministic derivation. Application keys are derived under explicit context strings, allowing the protocol to dedicate a distinct authentication key to MEV ordering without reusing keys from unrelated applications.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Context isolation. Keys derived under distinct contexts are computationally independent under the pseudorandomness of the underlying KDF.
texts.text: Context isolation. Keys derived under distinct contexts are computationally independent under the pseudorandomness of the underlying KDF.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Operational simplicity. Honest users manage one identity root while still obtaining application-specific signing keys, which lowers operational overhead without weakening protocol-level accountability.
texts.text: Operational simplicity. Honest users manage one identity root while still obtaining application-specific signing keys, which lowers operational overhead without weakening protocol-level accountability.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Post-quantum compatibility. ACE-GF can instantiate its authentication context with ML-DSA-44 (NIST FIPS 204), allowing the ordering protocol to inherit post-quantum signature support.
texts.text: Post-quantum compatibility. ACE-GF can instantiate its authentication context with ML-DSA-44 (NIST FIPS 204), allowing the ordering protocol to inherit post-quantum signature support.

texts.text: Definition 1 (MEV Authentication Key) . For a user with identity root REV , let

texts.orig: ( sk auth , vk auth ) = DeriveSigKey mev ( REV )

texts.label: text

texts.text: denote the signature key pair derived for MEV-ACE authentication.

texts.text: Definition 2 (Registered Identity Commitment) . For a user with authentication verification key vk auth , the public identity commitment used by MEV-ACE is

texts.label: formula
texts.orig: idcom = H ( vk auth ) ,

texts.label: text
texts.orig: where H is SHA-256. A registered economic identity is the on-chain tuple ( idcom , vk auth , D stake ) , where D stake is a slashable bond locked for protocol participation.
texts.text: where H is SHA-256. A registered economic identity is the on-chain tuple ( idcom , vk auth , D stake ) , where D stake is a slashable bond locked for protocol participation.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Remark 1. In MEV-ACE, protocol-level Sybil resistance does not rely on local wallet setup cost. The enforceable cost comes from the registered bond D stake , the per-slot commitment quota ℓ , and slashing for invalid behavior or selective non-opening. ACE-GF is used to make authenticated key derivation convenient and context-isolated, not to serve as the sole anti-Sybil mechanism.

The enforceable cost comes from the registered bond D stake , the per-slot commitment quota ℓ , and slashing for invalid behavior or selective non-opening. texts.orig: 2.2 Verifiable Delay Functions
texts.text: 2.2 Verifiable Delay Functions

texts.label: text
texts.orig: AVerifiable Delay Function (VDF) [10] is a function f : X → Y that requires T sequential computation steps to evaluate but whose output can be verified in O (log T ) time.
texts.text: AVerifiable Delay Function (VDF) [10] is a function f : X → Y that requires T sequential computation steps to evaluate but whose output can be verified in O (log T ) time.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Definition 3 (VDF) . A VDF scheme consists of three algorithms:
texts.text: Definition 3 (VDF) . A VDF scheme consists of three algorithms:

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Setup (1 λ , T ) → pp : generates public parameters for security parameter λ and delay parameter T .
texts.text: Setup (1 λ , T ) → pp : generates public parameters for security parameter λ and delay parameter T .

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Eval ( pp , x ) → ( y, π ) : computes output y with proof π in exactly T sequential steps.
texts.text: Eval ( pp , x ) → ( y, π ) : computes output y with proof π in exactly T sequential steps.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Verify ( pp , x, y, π ) →{ 0 , 1 } : verifies the output in O (log T ) time.
texts.text: Verify ( pp , x, y, π ) →{ 0 , 1 } : verifies the output in O (log T ) time.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We require the sequential computation assumption : no adversary with poly ( λ ) parallel processors can evaluate the VDF significantly faster than T sequential steps.
texts.text: We require the sequential computation assumption : no adversary with poly ( λ ) parallel processors can evaluate the VDF significantly faster than T sequential steps.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We define three properties that collectively eliminate unilateral proposer discretion over admitted transactions:
texts.text: We define three properties that collectively eliminate unilateral proposer discretion over admitted transactions:

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Definition 8 (Order-Unpredictability) . A fair-ordering protocol satisfies order-unpredictability if, for any PPT adversary A controlling the block producer, the probability that A can predict the position of any target executable transaction tx i in the final ordering, given all information available before the admissible commitment set is locked, satisfies:
texts.text: Definition 8 (Order-Unpredictability) . A fair-ordering protocol satisfies order-unpredictability if, for any PPT adversary A controlling the block producer, the probability that A can predict the position of any target executable transaction tx i in the final ordering, given all information available before the admissible commitment set is locked, satisfies:

texts.label: formula
texts.orig: Pr [ A predicts σ ( i ) ] ≤ 1 m + negl ( λ )

texts.label: text
texts.orig: where m is the number of executable openings in the slot.
texts.text: where m is the number of executable openings in the slot.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Definition 9 (Commitment Authenticity) . A fair-ordering protocol satisfies commitment authenticity if, for any PPT adversary A , the probability that A produces an admissible commitment for a registered identity idcom ∗ without possessing the corresponding authentication signing key is:
texts.text: Definition 9 (Commitment Authenticity) . A fair-ordering protocol satisfies commitment authenticity if, for any PPT adversary A , the probability that A produces an admissible commitment for a registered identity idcom ∗ without possessing the corresponding authentication signing key is:

texts.label: formula
texts.orig: Pr [ A outputs an admissible commitment for idcom ∗ without sk auth ∗ ] ≤ negl ( λ )

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Definition 10 (Accountable Inclusion) . A fair-ordering protocol satisfies accountable inclusion if any transaction that has both an admissible commitment and an executable opening must appear in every valid finalized block for that slot, and any omission can be proven by a publicly verifiable omission proof derived from ( R C i , R O i ) .
texts.text: Definition 10 (Accountable Inclusion) . A fair-ordering protocol satisfies accountable inclusion if any transaction that has both an admissible commitment and an executable opening must appear in every valid finalized block for that slot, and any omission can be proven by a publicly verifiable omission proof derived from ( R C i , R O i ) .

texts.orig: 4.1 Protocol Overview
texts.text: 4.1 Protocol Overview

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The protocol combines three ideas:
texts.text: The protocol combines three ideas:

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 1. Authenticated admissibility : a commitment is not merely a hash; it must be signed by a registered identity and acknowledged by a threshold of validators before it enters the admissible set.
texts.text: Authenticated admissibility : a commitment is not merely a hash; it must be signed by a registered identity and acknowledged by a threshold of validators before it enters the admissible set.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 2. Delayed randomness : the execution permutation is derived only after the admissible set is locked, removing the producer's ability to pick positions after learning transaction contents.
texts.text: Delayed randomness : the execution permutation is derived only after the admissible set is locked, removing the producer's ability to pick positions after learning transaction contents.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 3. Receipt-backed inclusion : both commitments and openings carry portable receipt bundles, so omission can be proven to any validator rather than inferred only by local observation.
texts.text: Receipt-backed inclusion : both commitments and openings carry portable receipt bundles, so omission can be proven to any validator rather than inferred only by local observation.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Together, these mechanisms constrain the producer in two dimensions: it cannot cheaply fabricate additional admissible demand, and it cannot silently omit a transaction that has already crossed the protocol's admission thresholds.
texts.text: Together, these mechanisms constrain the producer in two dimensions: it cannot cheaply fabricate additional admissible demand, and it cannot silently omit a transaction that has already crossed the protocol's admission thresholds.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Lemma 1 (Bounded Stuffing Cost) . Let ℓ be the maximum number of commitments allowed per identity per slot. To inject k additional admissible commitments in a slot beyond its currently registered capacity, an adversary must control at least ⌈ k/ℓ ⌉ additional active identities and therefore lock at least ⌈ k/ℓ ⌉ · D stake of slashable capital.
texts.text: Lemma 1 (Bounded Stuffing Cost) . Let ℓ be the maximum number of commitments allowed per identity per slot. To inject k additional admissible commitments in a slot beyond its currently registered capacity, an adversary must control at least ⌈ k/ℓ ⌉ additional active identities and therefore lock at least ⌈ k/ℓ ⌉ · D stake of slashable capital.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Proof. By protocol rule, each active identity can contribute at most ℓ admissible commitments to a slot. Therefore k extra admissible commitments require at least ⌈ k/ℓ ⌉ extra identities. Each such identity must be registered with an active bond D stake , so the minimum additional slashable capital is ⌈ k/ℓ ⌉ · D stake .

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 5.6 Security Boundaries and Limitations
texts.text: 5.6 Security Boundaries and Limitations

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We explicitly acknowledge the following limitations:
texts.text: We explicitly acknowledge the following limitations:

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · The guarantees apply only after receipt thresholds are met. If a user fails to collect R C i or R O i before the relevant cutoff, the transaction never becomes mandatory for that slot.
texts.text: The guarantees apply only after receipt thresholds are met. If a user fails to collect R C i or R O i before the relevant cutoff, the transaction never becomes mandatory for that slot.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Information-based and cross-domain MEV remain out of scope. Backrunning on public state changes and arbitrage across chains still require orthogonal mechanisms such as batch auctions or delayed disclosure [13].
texts.text: Information-based and cross-domain MEV remain out of scope. Backrunning on public state changes and arbitrage across chains still require orthogonal mechanisms such as batch auctions or delayed disclosure [13].

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · The protocol depends on timely dissemination of omission proofs. If valid omission proofs do not reach honest validators before they vote, accountable inclusion becomes weaker in practice even though the proof objects are sound.
texts.text: The protocol depends on timely dissemination of omission proofs. If valid omission proofs do not reach honest validators before they vote, accountable inclusion becomes weaker in practice even though the proof objects are sound.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · VDF hardware asymmetry remains a deployment risk. If one party can compute the VDF materially faster than the calibrated bound, delayed randomness may no longer provide the intended uncertainty window.
texts.text: VDF hardware asymmetry remains a deployment risk. If one party can compute the VDF materially faster than the calibrated bound, delayed randomness may no longer provide the intended uncertainty window.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Economic guarantees are parameter-sensitive. If D stake , δ user , or δ prod are set below realistic one-slot profit opportunities, selective non-opening or stuffing can become rational again.
texts.text: Economic guarantees are parameter-sensitive. If D stake , δ user , or δ prod are set below realistic one-slot profit opportunities, selective non-opening or stuffing can become rational again.

texts.text: 6 Performance Analysis

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The original version of this paper conflated cryptographic delay parameters, wall-clock runtime, and end-to-end slot overhead. We separate them here.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 6.1 Computation and Communication Costs
texts.text: 6.1 Computation and Communication Costs

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Let m be the number of admissible commitments in a slot and n = 3 f +1 the validator count. MEV-ACE adds four main cost components:
texts.text: Let m be the number of admissible commitments in a slot and n = 3 f +1 the validator count. MEV-ACE adds four main cost components:

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · User-side work: each transaction adds one commitment hash, one opening nonce, and one authentication signature.
texts.text: User-side work: each transaction adds one commitment hash, one opening nonce, and one authentication signature.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Validator-side work: commit and open admission require O ( m ) signature/hash checks over the slot.
texts.text: Validator-side work: commit and open admission require O ( m ) signature/hash checks over the slot.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Canonicalization: constructing the admissible-set root requires an O ( m log m ) sort and O ( m ) hashes.
texts.text: Canonicalization: constructing the admissible-set root requires an O ( m log m ) sort and O ( m ) hashes.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Delayed randomness: VDF evaluation is deployment-specific, while verification remains O (log T ) or better depending on the construction.
texts.text: Delayed randomness: VDF evaluation is deployment-specific, while verification remains O (log T ) or better depending on the construction.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Communication overhead arises from two additional user messages per transaction (commit and open) plus validator receipt certificates. In practice, this means MEV-ACE is bandwidth-sensitive unless receipt signatures are batched, aggregated, or compressed in the execution layer.
texts.text: Communication overhead arises from two additional user messages per transaction (commit and open) plus validator receipt certificates. In practice, this means MEV-ACE is bandwidth-sensitive unless receipt signatures are batched, aggregated, or compressed in the execution layer.

texts.text: ∆ c is the time for users to obtain commit certificates R C i ;

texts.text: ∆ v is the wall-clock delay introduced by the randomness mechanism;

texts.text: ∆ o is the time for users to obtain opening certificates R O i ;

A deployment can therefore be single-slot only if its chain-level slot duration is large enough to hold all three phases with margin.

texts.orig: 6.3 Throughput Implications
texts.text: 6.3 Throughput Implications

texts.label: text
texts.orig: MEV-ACE does not impose a fixed percentage throughput penalty independent of deployment. Its throughput impact depends on three variables:
texts.text: MEV-ACE does not impose a fixed percentage throughput penalty independent of deployment. Its throughput impact depends on three variables:

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 1. the validator count and signature scheme used for receipts;
texts.text: the validator count and signature scheme used for receipts;

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 2. whether receipt evidence is carried as individual signatures, aggregates, or committee certificates;
texts.text: whether receipt evidence is carried as individual signatures, aggregates, or committee certificates;

texts.label: list_item

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Consequently, a credible throughput claim requires an implementation benchmark with a concrete validator topology, signature scheme, aggregation strategy, and slot scheduler. This paper does not claim a universal 3-7 ms overhead or a fixed TPS number.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 6.4 Qualitative Comparison
texts.text: 6.4 Qualitative Comparison

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Relative to plain commit-reveal, MEV-ACE adds two capabilities that materially change the security boundary: authenticated admissibility and omission proofs. Relative to threshold-encryption systems, it avoids a decryption committee but still requires a carefully calibrated delay budget. Relative to PBS, it targets the source of ordering discretion rather than merely reallocating extracted value between builders and proposers.
texts.text: Relative to plain commit-reveal, MEV-ACE adds two capabilities that materially change the security boundary: authenticated admissibility and omission proofs. Relative to threshold-encryption systems, it avoids a decryption committee but still requires a carefully calibrated delay budget. Relative to PBS, it targets the source of ordering discretion rather than merely reallocating extracted value between builders and proposers.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 7 Discussion
texts.text: 7 Discussion

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 7.1 Why Identity Binding Is the Missing Piece
texts.text: 7.1 Why Identity Binding Is the Missing Piece

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The core observation of this work is more precise than 'identity solves MEV.' What matters is authenticated admissibility backed by slashable economic identities . Fair ordering without authenticated admission still lets the producer manufacture demand; authenticated admission without delayed randomness still lets the producer condition reveals on realized order. MEV-ACE pairs these two controls and adds receipt-backed inclusion so omission becomes globally auditable.
texts.text: The core observation of this work is more precise than 'identity solves MEV.' What matters is authenticated admissibility backed by slashable economic identities . Fair ordering without authenticated admission still lets the producer manufacture demand; authenticated admission without delayed randomness still lets the producer condition reveals on realized order. MEV-ACE pairs these two controls and adds receipt-backed inclusion so omission becomes globally auditable.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 7.2 Generalization Beyond ACE-GF
texts.text: 7.2 Generalization Beyond ACE-GF

texts.label: text
texts.orig: While MEV-ACE is presented in the context of ACE-GF, the design generalizes to any system that provides:
texts.text: While MEV-ACE is presented in the context of ACE-GF, the design generalizes to any system that provides:

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 1. A registered verification key or equivalent public credential for protocol authentication.
texts.text: A registered verification key or equivalent public credential for protocol authentication.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 2. A slashable participation bond or other enforceable cost for expanding identity capacity.
texts.text: A slashable participation bond or other enforceable cost for expanding identity capacity.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 3. A way to dedicate protocol-specific signing material so that ordering authentication is isolated from other applications.
texts.text: A way to dedicate protocol-specific signing material so that ordering authentication is isolated from other applications.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: ACE-GF is attractive because it supplies item (3) cleanly and can instantiate the authentication key with post-quantum signatures. The security proofs in this paper, however, ultimately rely on registered keys, receipt thresholds, and bonded participation rather than on any ACE-GF-specific local wallet workflow.

The security proofs in this paper, however, ultimately rely on registered keys, receipt thresholds, and bonded participation rather than on any ACE-GF-specific local wallet workflow.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 7.3 Parameter Selection Guidelines
texts.text: 7.3 Parameter Selection Guidelines

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Receipt thresholds q c , q o : Set to at least 2 f +1 so that every certificate contains signatures from at least f +1 honest validators and can therefore support omission proofs.
texts.text: Receipt thresholds q c , q o : Set to at least 2 f +1 so that every certificate contains signatures from at least f +1 honest validators and can therefore support omission proofs.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Delay budget ∆ v : Choose ∆ v to exceed the producer's maximum timing advantage in learning the final admissible-set root, with additional margin for hardware asymmetry and network jitter.
texts.text: Delay budget ∆ v : Choose ∆ v to exceed the producer's maximum timing advantage in learning the final admissible-set root, with additional margin for hardware asymmetry and network jitter.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Stake deposit D stake : Size the user bond so that δ user D stake exceeds the maximum gain from withholding one admitted opening, and ⌈ k/ℓ ⌉ D stake exceeds the gain from stuffing k extra commitments.
texts.text: Stake deposit D stake : Size the user bond so that δ user D stake exceeds the maximum gain from withholding one admitted opening, and ⌈ k/ℓ ⌉ D stake exceeds the gain from stuffing k extra commitments.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Producer bond B prod : Size the producer-side slash so that δ prod B prod exceeds the maximum one-slot benefit from invalid ordering or omission.
texts.text: Producer bond B prod : Size the producer-side slash so that δ prod B prod exceeds the maximum one-slot benefit from invalid ordering or omission.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Rate limit ℓ : Choose ℓ based on expected user activity. A larger ℓ improves UX for high-frequency users but weakens the capital cost of commitment stuffing.
texts.text: Rate limit ℓ : Choose ℓ based on expected user activity. A larger ℓ improves UX for high-frequency users but weakens the capital cost of commitment stuffing.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 8 Related Work
texts.text: 8 Related Work

texts.label: text
texts.orig: MEVtaxonomy and measurement. Daian et al. [2] introduced the concept of miner extractable value and documented front-running on Ethereum. Flashbots later published MEV-Explore as a practical measurement resource for extracted MEV [3]. Qin et al. [4] quantified extractable value across major DeFi activity classes. Together, this line of work shows that ordering-based MEV is a structural problem in blockchain systems where transaction ordering affects economic outcomes.

Flashbots later published MEV-Explore as a practical measurement resource for extracted MEV [3]. Qin et al. [4] quantified extractable value across major DeFi activity classes. Together, this line of work shows that ordering-based MEV is a structural problem in blockchain systems where transaction ordering affects economic outcomes.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Commit-reveal and encryption-based approaches. Canidio and Danos [5] analyze commit-reveal schemes against front-running. Shutter Network [6] uses threshold encryption to hide transaction contents from sequencers. These approaches reduce early information leakage, but by themselves they do not authenticate admissible commitments via bonded identities or provide portable omission proofs.

Shutter Network [6] uses threshold encryption to hide transaction contents from sequencers. These approaches reduce early information leakage, but by themselves they do not authenticate admissible commitments via bonded identities or provide portable omission proofs.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Fair ordering services. Kelkar et al. [9] formalized order-fairness and proposed Aequitas. Chainlink Fair Sequencing Services (FSS) [7] decentralizes ordering via oracle networks. These require external trust assumptions that MEV-ACE avoids.
texts.text: Fair ordering services. Kelkar et al. [9] formalized order-fairness and proposed Aequitas. Chainlink Fair Sequencing Services (FSS) [7] decentralizes ordering via oracle networks. These require external trust assumptions that MEV-ACE avoids.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Proposer-builder separation. Flashbots MEV-Boost [8] separates block building from proposing in Ethereum. This is a redistribution mechanism (the builder still extracts MEV; the proposer receives a share), not an elimination mechanism.
texts.text: Proposer-builder separation. Flashbots MEV-Boost [8] separates block building from proposing in Ethereum. This is a redistribution mechanism (the builder still extracts MEV; the proposer receives a share), not an elimination mechanism.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: VDF-based protocols. Boneh et al. [10] formalized VDFs. Wesolowski [11] provided efficient VDF constructions. MEV-ACE uses delayed randomness not as a standalone fairness primitive, but as one component in a larger admissibility-and-inclusion framework.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Identity and Sybil resistance. Proof-of-personhood protocols [12] address Sybil resistance through identity verification. ACE-GF [1] provides a cryptographic identity framework with context-isolated key derivation. MEV-ACE differs from both strands by using registered, slashable protocol identities to authenticate commitments and openings in the block-building path.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 9 Conclusion
texts.text: 9 Conclusion

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Wehave presented MEV-ACE, an identity-authenticated fair-ordering protocol for proposercontrolled MEV mitigation. The central claim is narrower and stronger than the original draft: once commitments and openings cross threshold receipt gates, the producer no longer retains unilateral discretion to reorder or omit them without either violating verifiable protocol rules or paying explicitly modeled economic penalties. This result follows from the combination of registered authentication keys, receipt-backed admissibility, delayed randomness, and slashable participation bonds.
texts.text: Wehave presented MEV-ACE, an identity-authenticated fair-ordering protocol for proposercontrolled MEV mitigation. The central claim is narrower and stronger than the original draft: once commitments and openings cross threshold receipt gates, the producer no longer retains unilateral discretion to reorder or omit them without either violating verifiable protocol rules or paying explicitly modeled economic penalties. This result follows from the combination of registered authentication keys, receipt-backed admissibility, delayed randomness, and slashable participation bonds.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The paper's security conclusion is therefore conditional rather than rhetorical. MEVACE provides order-unpredictability, commitment authenticity, and accountable inclusion under standard cryptographic assumptions, timely dissemination of omission proofs, and correctly sized economic parameters. ACE-GF remains useful as the deterministic identity/key-derivation substrate and preserves compatibility with post-quantum signatures such as ML-DSA-44, but the enforceable security boundary is the protocol itself.
texts.text: The paper's security conclusion is therefore conditional rather than rhetorical. MEVACE provides order-unpredictability, commitment authenticity, and accountable inclusion under standard cryptographic assumptions, timely dissemination of omission proofs, and correctly sized economic parameters. ACE-GF remains useful as the deterministic identity/key-derivation substrate and preserves compatibility with post-quantum signatures such as ML-DSA-44, but the enforceable security boundary is the protocol itself.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Future work. The next three priorities are: (1) benchmarking concrete receipt and VDF implementations under realistic validator topologies; (2) formalizing omission-proof handling inside a full BFT state machine; and (3) studying how this framework composes with batch auctions or encrypted execution for information-based MEV.
